perturbation.original_text: (t) "Derivative Works" (i) with respect to copyrightable works shall have the meaning set forth in the U.S. Copyright Act, 17 U.S.C. §101, et seq. (including translation into other computer language and any other form in which an existing work may be recast, transformed or adapted which would constitute a derivative work under the U.S. Copyright Act, 17 U.S.C. §101), and (ii) with respect to Know-How, it shall also mean any material derived from such Know-How.
perturbation.changed_text: (t) "Derivative Works" (i) with respect to copyrightable works shall have the meaning that the parties commonly ascribe to it, irrespective of the U.S. Copyright Act, 17 U.S.C. §101, et seq., and (ii) with respect to Know-How, it shall also mean any material derived from such Know-How.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that the definition of "Derivative Works" is irrespective of the U.S. Copyright Act, the contract creates a contradiction, as derivative works have a specific legal meaning under copyright law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the Copyright Act by explicitly stating the definition of derivative work is independent of the statutory definition. This creates uncertainty because the contract now uses a definition that may not align with legal standards, affecting copyright enforcement and interpretation of rights.

perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly states that the definition of 'Derivative Works' is irrespective of the U.S. Copyright Act, 17 U.S.C. §101, et seq. This directly contradicts the original contract's reliance on the statutory definition. Scraped Law Snippet 1, which contains 17 USC 101, the Definitions section of the Copyright Act, emphasizes that definitions within the title are governed by the Act unless otherwise provided. By deliberately disregarding the statutory definition, the changed text creates a legal contradiction as the contractual definition of derivative work could then deviate significantly from the legally established meaning, leading to uncertainty and potential unenforceability regarding copyright-related provisions.

perturbation.original_text: (kk) "Intellectual Property" means any and all of the following in any jurisdiction throughout the world: (i) patents, patent applications, industrial design rights (including utility model rights, design rights, and industrial property rights), registrations and applications for registration of industrial design rights, patents of importation/confirmation, statutory invention registrations, and certificates of invention and like statutory right, all counterparts, continuations, divisions, continuations-in-part, revisions, extensions, supplementary certificates, substitutions, reexaminations, renewals, and reissuances of any of the foregoing and any patent or patent application that claims priority to any of the foregoing (collectively, "Patents"); (ii) Trademarks; (iii) copyright registrations and applications, and unregistered copyrights, published and unpublished works of authorship, and Mask Works, together with all applications, registrations, and renewals in connection therewith, and all common-law rights to any of the foregoing (collectively, "Copyrights"); (iv) computer programs, whether embodied in software, firmware or otherwise, including, software compilations, software implementations of algorithms, software tool sets, compilers, and software models and methodologies (whether in Source Code or Object Code form), and (C) translation, ported
perturbation.changed_text: (kk) "Intellectual Property" means any and all of the following in any jurisdiction throughout the world, *excluding patents and copyrights*: (iv) computer programs, whether embodied in software, firmware or otherwise, including, software compilations, software implementations of algorithms, software tool sets, compilers, and software models and methodologies (whether in Source Code or Object Code form), and (C) translation, ported
perturbation.explanation: By excluding patents and copyrights from the definition of 'Intellectual Property', the contract now directly contradicts established legal definitions of IP, which includes patents and copyrights. This affects the scope of the entire agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification directly contradicts established U.S. law, specifically the Patent Act and the Copyright Act, which define patents and copyrights as key components of intellectual property. Excluding them creates confusion and renders several clauses of the contract potentially unenforceable as they relate to patents and copyrights.

perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly excludes 'patents and copyrights' from the definition of 'Intellectual Property.' This directly contradicts established legal precedent, as confirmed by the LLM's Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 35 USC 101, affirms that inventions can be patented, thereby making patents a component of intellectual property, which the modified contract text excludes. This exclusion creates a significant contradiction with the law, as it alters the scope of intellectual property rights within the contract, rendering related clauses potentially unenforceable. Therefore, a legal contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: (s) "Copyrights" has the meaning ascribed to it in the definition of Intellectual Property.
perturbation.explanation: By limiting "Copyrights" to only registered copyrights, the definition deviates from established copyright law, which recognizes unregistered copyrights.
perturbation.law_explanation: This conflicts with the Copyright Act because copyright protection exists automatically upon creation of a work, regardless of registration. This affects the scope of licensed materials and enforcement rights.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: copyright | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you copyright Copyright is the exclusive legal right to reproduce, publish, sell, or distribute the matter and form of something. Overview - U.S. Copyright Act The U.S. Copyright Act, (codified at 17 U.S.C. §§ 101 - 810 ), is federal legislation which protects the writings of authors. Note that advancements in technology have led to an ever expanding understanding of the word "writings." The Copyright Act now reaches architectural design, software, the graphic arts, motion pictures, and sound recordings and more. All works of authorship fixed in a tangible medium of expression and within the subject matter of copyright were deemed to fall within the exclusive jurisdiction of the Copyright Act regardless of whether the work was created before or after that date and whether published or unpublished. Under § 102 , copyright protection exists in original works of authorship fixed in any tangible medium of expression from which they can be perceived, reproduced, or otherwise communicated, either directly or with the aid of a machine or device. Derivative Works Under § 106 , the owner of a copyright has the exclusive right to reproduce, distribute, perform, display, license, and to prepare derivative works based on the copyrighted work. Fair Works Under § 107 , the exclusive rights of the copyright owner are subject to limitation by the doctrine of " fair use ." Fair use of a copyrighted work for purposes such as criticism, comment, news reporting, teaching, scholarship, or research is not copyright infringement. To determine whether a particular use qualifies as fair use, courts apply the multi-factor balancing test from § 107 . Limits on Copyright Protection Copyright protection does not extend to any idea, procedure, process, system, method of operation, concept, principle, or discovery. For example, if a book is written describing a new system of bookkeeping, copyright protection only extends to the author's description of the bookkeeping system; it does not protect the system itself. This view was expressed in Baker v. Selden, 101 U.S. 99 (1879) . In addition to being independently created by the author, to qualify for copyright protection a work must also exhibit a minimum of originality. In Feist Publications v. Rural Telephone Service, 499 U.S. 340 (1991) , the Supreme Court held that a work must have "some minimal degree of creativity." Thus, a mere alphabetical list of data is not protected, but other original aspects of the work (page layout, design, format, or even the specific selection of data points) might be. This underscores the idea that information itself is not copyrightable, only the specific arrangements or presentations of it. Copyright Registration According to the Copyright Act of 1976, registration of copyright is voluntary and may take place at any
perturbation.justification: The modification creates a contradiction because it limits the definition of "Copyrights" to only registered copyrights. The Law Explanation clarifies that copyright protection exists automatically upon creation of a work, regardless of registration, which the snippet from Wex supports. The original text aligns with this by referencing the broader definition of Intellectual Property, while the changed text narrows this scope, conflicting with the established legal principle of automatic copyright upon creation.
